sections.heading: Laser Alignment Overview
sections.content: The Laser Alignment tab (tab_laser.py) is where you calibrate the mapping between camera pixels and physical servo angles. By clicking on the laser dot in the camera feed and recording the corresponding servo angles, you build a set of correspondence points. From these, NEXUS computes a homography matrix H using RANSAC that can accurately transform any detected target pixel coordinate into the exact pan/tilt command needed to aim the laser at that target.

sections.heading: Why Laser Alignment is Required
sections.content: The relationship between "where a pixel appears in the camera" and "what servo angle points the laser at that pixel" is not a simple linear formula. Lens distortion, camera mounting angle, servo mechanical offsets, and physical geometry all contribute non-linear effects. Homography captures all of these effects as a single 3×3 matrix that maps any pixel to the correct servo angle — including regions between calibration points.
list: Without laser alignment: The engine uses a raw linear pixel-to-angle approximation that can be off by 10–30° depending on target position in the frame
With laser alignment: The engine uses the homography H for sub-degree accuracy across the entire camera field of view
Complementary to stereo calibration: Stereo calibration gives depth; laser alignment gives direction. Both are required for a fully calibrated system

sections.heading: Alignment Workflow
steps: Go to the 🔵 LASER ALIGNMENT tab
Ensure ESP32 is connected — check HW status (must be green / LINKED)
Click START PREVIEW — the left camera feed appears in the panel
Enable the laser via the LASER ON button or the Hardware Control tab
Use the Manual Pan slider and Manual Tilt slider to steer the laser to a visible point on a flat surface (wall, target board)
When the laser dot is clearly visible and stable, click on the laser dot in the camera feed — this records pixel coordinates (cx, cy)
The current servo angles are automatically read from the ESP32 heartbeat/ACK and recorded as the corresponding (pan, tilt) pair
Move the laser to a new position (different area of the frame) and repeat
Collect at least 12 correspondence points — 20+ recommended for best accuracy
Click COMPUTE ALIGNMENT to run cv2.findHomography() with RANSAC
Review the reprojection error — target < 2.0 px
Restart the engine (or click RECONNECT) for the new homography to take effect

sections.heading: Correspondence Point Collection
sections.content: Each time you click on the laser dot in the camera feed, the system records one correspondence pair:
sections.code:
# In tab_laser.py — click handler on TacticalVideoWidget
def on_video_click(self, x: int, y: int):
    """Called when user clicks on the camera feed."""
    # Get current servo angles from hardware link
    current_pan, current_tilt = self.engine.hardware.get_current_angles()

    # Record the correspondence
    self.src_points.append([x, y])               # Pixel coordinate
    self.dst_points.append([current_pan, current_tilt])  # Servo angles

    # Visual feedback: draw a dot at the click position
    self.point_overlay.append((x, y, current_pan, current_tilt))

    # Update point count display
    self.point_count_label.setText(
        f"Points: {len(self.src_points)} / 12 minimum"
    )
    self.log(f"Point {len(self.src_points)}: pixel=({x},{y}) → pan={current_pan:.1f}° tilt={current_tilt:.1f}°")

sections.heading: Homography Computation
sections.content: Once you have 12+ points and click COMPUTE ALIGNMENT, the system calls:
sections.code:
# From core/math_calib.py
import cv2, numpy as np

@staticmethod
def calculate_homography(
    src: np.ndarray,   # Shape (N,2) — pixel coords [cx, cy]
    dst: np.ndarray    # Shape (N,2) — servo angles [pan, tilt]
) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Compute a 3×3 homography matrix mapping pixel → servo angles.
    Uses RANSAC to reject outlier points automatically.
    """
    H, mask = cv2.findHomography(
        src, dst,
        method=cv2.RANSAC,
        ransacReprojThreshold=5.0  # Reject points > 5px from predicted position
    )
    inlier_count = int(mask.sum())
    print(f"Homography computed: {inlier_count}/{len(src)} inliers")
    return H

# Applying H during inference:
@staticmethod
def apply_homography(px: float, py: float, H: np.ndarray) -> tuple:
    point  = np.array([[[px, py]]], dtype=np.float32)
    result = cv2.perspectiveTransform(point, H)
    pan  = float(result[0][0][0])
    tilt = float(result[0][0][1])
    return pan, tilt

sections.heading: Coverage Strategy — Point Placement
sections.content: The spatial distribution of your calibration points directly determines accuracy across the frame. Poor coverage = large errors in uncovered regions.
rows: Homography extrapolation is worst at edges — corners anchor the matrix
Most detections happen near center — higher density here improves precision
Below 12, RANSAC may produce numerically unstable homography
More points → better RANSAC inlier selection → lower reprojection error

sections.heading: Visual Coverage Map
sections.code:
# Ideal 16-point distribution across a 1280×720 frame:
#
#  ×──────────×──────────×──────────×
#  │  corner  │   edge   │   edge   │  ← row 1 (y ≈ 100px)
#  ×──────────×──────────×──────────×
#  │   edge   │  center  │  center  │  ← row 2 (y ≈ 300px)
#  │          │  center  │  center  │  ← row 3 (y ≈ 420px)
#  ×──────────×──────────×──────────×
#  │  corner  │   edge   │  corner  │  ← row 4 (y ≈ 620px)
#  ×──────────×──────────×──────────×
#
# × = calibration point
# Horizontal coverage: ~100px, ~426px, ~853px, ~1180px
# Vertical coverage:   ~100px, ~300px, ~420px, ~620px

sections.heading: Reprojection Error
sections.content: After computing the homography, NEXUS reports the mean reprojection error — the average pixel distance between where each calibration point actually is and where the computed H predicts it should be:
rows: Consider adding more points, especially at frame edges
Likely has outlier points or poor coverage — delete outliers and recompute

sections.heading: What Gets Saved
sections.code:
# laser_alignment.npz contents (saved to data/ folder)
np.savez(
    'data/laser_alignment.npz',
    H                 = H,                 # (3,3) float64 — the homography matrix
    src_points        = src_arr,            # (N,2) — pixel coords used
    dst_points        = dst_arr,            # (N,2) — servo angles used
    point_count       = len(src_points),    # int   — number of correspondence pairs
    reprojection_error= reproj_err          # float — mean pixel reprojection error
)

# To inspect saved alignment:
import numpy as np
data = np.load('data/laser_alignment.npz')
print("Homography H:")
print(data['H'])
print(f"Built from {data['point_count']} points")
print(f"Reprojection error: {data['reprojection_error']:.2f} px")

sections.heading: Delete Individual Points
sections.content: If you accidentally click in the wrong position, you can remove individual correspondence points before computing the homography. The Laser Alignment tab shows a list of all recorded points — select any entry and click DELETE POINT to remove it. Always review the list before clicking COMPUTE ALIGNMENT.
sections.alertContent: RANSAC automatically rejects outlier points during computation, so a few bad clicks won't necessarily ruin the result. However, deliberately removing obvious outliers before computing always improves the final matrix quality.

sections.heading: Trim vs Full Realignment
sections.content: After laser alignment, small residual errors can be corrected with the Trim sliders in the Dashboard. However, Trim is a global offset — it shifts every servo command by a fixed amount. It cannot correct non-linear errors across the frame.
rows: Laser off by < 2° consistently across all positions
Use PAN_TRIM / TILT_TRIM sliders in Dashboard (±18° range)
Laser accurate at center but drifts at edges
Redo full laser alignment — poor coverage or few points
Redo full laser alignment — camera or mount was moved
Trim at maximum (±18°) and still not correcting
Redo full laser alignment — large systematic error in H

sections.heading: When to Redo Laser Alignment
list: The pan/tilt assembly was physically repositioned or bumped relative to the cameras
Servo endpoint angles were changed in the ESP32 firmware
The laser module was replaced or repositioned on the mount
The camera mounting position changed (even slight shifts affect the pixel-to-angle mapping)
Trim sliders are maxed out and the laser is still not accurate

sections.heading: Integration with VisionEngine
sections.content: Once saved, the laser alignment file is loaded automatically by the engine at startup via setup_matrices():
sections.code:
# In vision_engine.py — setup_matrices()
try:
    laser_data = np.load(CONFIG['LASER_FILE'])
    self.H = laser_data['H']
    self.has_laser_cal = True
    logger.info(f"Laser homography loaded — "
                f"{int(laser_data['point_count'])} points, "
                f"reproj_error={laser_data['reprojection_error']:.1f}px")
except FileNotFoundError:
    self.H = None
    self.has_laser_cal = False
    logger.warning("No laser_alignment.npz found — using raw pixel-to-angle mapping")

# Each frame: pixel coords → servo angles
if self.H is not None:
    pan, tilt = CalibrationMath.apply_homography(smooth_cx, smooth_cy, self.H)
else:
    # Fallback: linear interpolation (much less accurate)
    pan  = (smooth_cx / frame_width)  * 180.0
    tilt = (smooth_cy / frame_height) * 180.0
